ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry exists for Vietnamese mango salad with pork and prawns. FOODON_03301710 (salad food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for mixed salad dishes is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Vietnamese mango salad with pork and prawns.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese salad dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of mango salad with pork and prawns is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a shared starter or individual main salad plate.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient values estimated from component ingredients: green mango (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169910), cooked pork (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 167903), cooked shrimp/prawns (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180), fish sauce (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173580), and standard Vietnamese salad dressing components. Weighted composite calculation based on typical recipe proportions: 40% green mango, 25% pork, 20% prawns, 15% dressing and garnish.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Goi Xoai Tom Thit found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data using a weighted composite of typical recipe proportions. Energy calculated using Atwater factors. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a typical restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous foods: green mango GI approximately 51 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283); combined dish GI weighted by carbohydrate contribution of mango component (dominant carbohydrate source) and protein/fat moderating effect of pork and prawns.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Goi Xoai Tom Thit. GI of 51 estimated using green mango as the primary carbohydrate source (GI ~51, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care). The protein from pork and prawns and fat content further moderate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (51 × 10.5 × 3) / 100 = 16.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a typical 300 g serving of this dish may contribute approximately 1,260 mg sodium, primarily from fish sauce, and should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from green mango and added sugar in dressing
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 51 (low-moderate) and a glycemic load of approximately 16 per serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, as the natural sugars in green mango and added sugar in the dressing can contribute meaningfully to postprandial glucose; the protein from pork and prawns helps moderate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium from fish sauce and cholesterol from prawns and pork
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the moderate sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (~55 mg/100 g) from prawns and pork; however, the dish also provides beneficial vitamin C, potassium, and lean protein, and can be part of a heart-healthy diet when consumed in controlled portions with reduced fish sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goi Xoai Tom Thit (Gỏi Xoài Tôm Thịt) is a Vietnamese green mango salad featuring shredded unripe mango tossed with sliced boiled pork and cooked prawns, dressed in a tangy-sweet-spicy Vietnamese dressing made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and fresh chilli. The dish is garnished with roasted peanuts, fried shallots, and fresh herbs such as Vietnamese coriander (rau ram) and mint, providing contrasting textures of crunchy mango, tender meat, and crispy garnishes. Originating from Vietnamese culinary tradition, this salad is popular as a starter or light main course in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore, including establishments such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate protein content (~7.8 g/100 g) from pork and prawns, a relatively low fat content (~3.2 g/100 g), and a moderate carbohydrate content (~10.5 g/100 g) dominated by the natural sugars of green mango. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 51 (low-moderate), moderated by the protein and fat content. Sodium is a notable concern at approximately 420 mg/100 g due to fish sauce. The dish is rich in vitamin C from mango (~18 mg/100 g) and provides useful amounts of potassium, iron, and B vitamins. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish or peanut allergies. Clinically, it is a moderate-concern dish for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes due to sodium and sugar content, but can be adapted by reducing fish sauce and sugar in the dressing.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Goi Xoai Tom Thit was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data using weighted recipe proportions. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (green mango) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach rather than a direct laboratory or database match.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component data (green mango FDC 169910, cooked pork FDC 167903, cooked shrimp FDC 175180, fish sauce FDC 173580) using approximate recipe proportions of 40% green mango, 25% pork, 20% prawns, 15% dressing and garnish. Actual restaurant values may vary significantly depending on dressing quantity, sugar added, and portion composition. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported for this dish type in the component source databases and is not a characteristic nutrient of this food. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) as none of the primary ingredients contain industrially produced trans fats. The dish is classified under food_group 'Mixed' rather than 'Vegetables' (as in the input seed record) because it contains substantial protein from pork and prawns. The cuisine is classified as Vietnamese rather than Singaporean, as the dish originates from Vietnamese culinary tradition and is served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore. Multilingual names for Singaporean context are provided (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) in addition to the Vietnamese native name, as the dish is served in Singapore. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (51 × 10.5 × 300) / 10000 = 16.1 g for a 300 g serving.
